symbols: bound figures tied to posts — the martyrdom of Sebastian, tied and shot with arrows
multiple views — the artist studying the suffering body from different angles
small seated figures below — mourners or attendants witnessing the martyrdom
handwritten notes — the working process of the artist preparing for a painting
description: A pen-and-ink sketch sheet showing multiple figure studies. Two large male nudes are bound to posts or stakes, their arms raised and bodies contorted in pain — studies for a Saint Sebastian martyrdom. At lower left, small sketches of seated or crouching figures. At lower right, additional small figure studies. Handwritten notes and numbers appear at upper left. The drawing is loose and gestural, clearly a preparatory study exploring different poses and compositions.
theological_description: Titian's preparatory sketches for a Saint Sebastian composition explore the physical reality of martyrdom through multiple poses of the bound, arrow-pierced saint. While Sebastian is not a biblical figure, his story embodies the apostolic call to suffer for Christ. Paul wrote 'I bear on my body the marks of Jesus' (Gal 6:17), and the suffering depicted in these studies evokes the psalmist's cry: 'Many are the afflictions of the righteous, but the Lord delivers him out of them all' (Ps 34:19). The multiple studies of the tortured body reflect the theological meditation on bodily suffering as participation in Christ's Passion — 'I am filling up what is lacking in Christ's afflictions for the sake of his body, the church' (Col 1:24). The small attendant figures suggest witnesses to the martyrdom, recalling Stephen's stoning where witnesses watched as the first Christian martyr died (Acts 7:58).

scripture_refs.reason: I bear on my body the marks of Jesus — the wounded body of the martyr

Filling up what is lacking in Christ's afflictions — martyrdom as participation in Christ's suffering
